nativeRange.habitat: Mixed coral, sand, and rubble of exposed outer reef flats, lagoons, and seaward reefs. Adults forage actively over open rubble flats turning shells and small stones for hidden prey; juveniles drift in shallow rubble and live algae near shelter.

decorPreferences: deep fine sand bed of at least 7-10 cm for night-time burrowing
open swimming space along the front of the tank
stable, well-anchored rockwork the fish cannot tip over
loose rubble and shells to forage and overturn
tightly fitting lid to prevent jumping

diet.notes: An obligate carnivore; in the wild it has been documented feeding heavily on molluscs, small crustaceans, hermit crabs, and occasionally tunicates. In captivity it generally takes a varied diet of frozen mysis, enriched brine shrimp, finely chopped marine meats (clam, krill, shrimp), and quality marine pellets. Multiple small feedings per day suit its high-activity foraging style.

avoidWith: other Coris wrasses in the same tank
very small or timid fish (gobies, dartfish)

imageCandidates.notes: Diego Delso, 5213x3475. Tack-sharp, vibrant in-situ transitional adult showing the trade-name look: dense blue spots, brilliant yellow caudal fin, green face lines; whole fish framed. Best diagnostic and quality pick.
Rickard Zerpe, 1803x1352. Sharp, vivid juvenile phase: bright red-orange body with white, black-edged saddles; whole fish in frame. Shows the dramatic juvenile coloration described in the summary.

Leonard Low, 2560x1576. Sharp full lateral of a mature adult showing the blue-green body with yellow caudal fin; colour slightly muted but uniquely represents the fully-adult phase distinct from the two other picks.

summary: A large, very active Indo-Pacific wrasse with a striking juvenile-to-adult colour change — bright red-orange with white spots as a juvenile, blue-green with a yellow tail as an adult. Best kept in big reef-style tanks with a deep sand bed.
careNotes: Coris gaimard is a large, long-lived wrasse that combines two demanding husbandry traits: a fine deep sand bed for nightly burrowing and a very large adult footprint. Juveniles are commonly sold at 5-8 cm and look like a completely different species from adults — vivid red-orange with white, black-edged spots. Adults reach roughly 25-38 cm and develop the blue-green body and bright yellow caudal fin that give the species its trade name; the two colour phases are sometimes mistaken for separate species in the trade. A fine sand bed of at least 7-10 cm is essential — the fish dives into the substrate to sleep and when alarmed, and has been reported to bash itself against the glass or refuse to settle without one. A tightly fitting lid is non-negotiable, as wrasses in this genus are well known to jump from open-top aquariums. Tank-size advice in the trade varies widely (sources suggest anything from 75 to 125 US gallons), but those figures are routinely given for juveniles only; a fish that will reach roughly 38 cm needs a long-footprint tank in the 750-1000 L range to live out its life. The species is generally considered not fully reef-safe — it will not eat coral polyps, but it has been reported to pick at ornamental shrimp, small snails, feather dusters, hermit crabs, and other small mobile invertebrates, and its rock-overturning foraging can dislodge frags. Temperament is usually shy and nervous when young but becomes increasingly territorial with age, particularly toward other wrasses. Like most labrids, Coris gaimard is protogynous (female-first sequential hermaphrodite).
breedingNotes: A pelagic spawner that forms distinct pairs during reproduction in the wild, releasing eggs and sperm into the water column. The planktonic larval phase has not been bridged in home aquaria; captive breeding is not established.

primary.name: FishBase
primary.notes: Designated LiveAquaria primary URL returned 404; FishBase used as primary in its place. Source for taxonomy (Labridae, Eupercaria), max length (40 cm TL, commonly ~20 cm), depth range (0-50 m), distribution (Pacific from Christmas/Cocos-Keeling east to Society and Tuamotu islands, north to Japan and Hawaii, south to Australia), wild diet (molluscs, crabs, hermit crabs, tunicates, forams), and wild temperature range (24.3-29 °C, mean 27.9 °C).

additional.name: AquariumSource — Red Coris Wrasse care guide
additional.notes: Secondary husbandry source used after LiveAquaria 404. Confirms adult size (6 in to over 12 in), tank size (100-125 gal single, 150 gal pair), temp 74-80 °F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.020-1.025, dKH 8-12, sand bed 2-4 in for burrowing, high jumping risk, lifespan 5-8 yr, juvenile-to-adult colour change, and not-fully-reef-safe status.

additional.name: ReefGuide.org — Yellowtail Coris
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size 10-15 in (25-38 cm) and Pacific distribution (Hawaii, Indonesia, GBR, French Polynesia). No husbandry parameters on this page.

additional.name: AquaMarinePower — Red Coris Wrasse Complete Guide
additional.notes: Third source for tank-size sanity check (minimum 75-125 gal in the trade; species needs upgrading as it grows). Used to anchor the conservative-larger 800 L recommendation per playbook tank-size rule.

fish.breedingNotes: Coris gaimard is a pelagic spawner that releases eggs and sperm into the water column. The planktonic larval stage has not been successfully completed in home aquaria, and captive breeding is not established.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Adults reach roughly 38 cm and need a long-footprint tank in the 750-1000 L range with a deep fine sand bed; juveniles are widely sold at 5-8 cm and are commonly under-housed, leading to chronic stress and stunting.
